result:
Here is a 2500 word piece of creative writing:

The old man sat on his front porch, watching the sun slowly descend below the horizon. The oranges and pinks of the sunset sky reflected in his weathered eyes as he listened to the crickets begin their evening symphony. He had witnessed this scene play out thousands of times over the decades, but it never failed to fill him with a sense of peace and contentment.  

As the first stars began to twinkle overhead, the old man's mind drifted to earlier times. He thought back to his childhood, growing up on a small farm not far from where he now sat. Life was simpler then. Days were spent doing chores, exploring the woods and fields with his brothers, coming home to the warm smile and home-cooked meals of his mother. His father was a strict but loving man who taught his sons the value of hard work, honesty, and family. 

The old man smiled as he remembered the mischief he and his brothers would get into - sneaking frogs into their sisters' beds, "borrowing" the neighbor's wagon for ill-fated joy rides down the big hill, coming home covered in mud and burrs but grinning from ear to ear. His mother would scold them but her eyes always sparkled with amusement.

Those carefree days seemed to stretch on forever, an endless summer of bare feet, scraped knees, and belly laughs. But of course, childhood never lasts. With the passing years came more responsibility, and the boy grew into a young man. He finished school, worked on the farm alongside his father and brothers, and began to dream about the future.

When the war came, the young man watched many of his friends ship off to fight on distant shores. He wanted to go as well, to serve his country, but his father insisted he was needed on the farm. The young man reluctantly agreed, but part of him envied the adventure and glory the soldiers were surely experiencing. Little did he know the reality of the battlefield, the horror and carnage that would scar so many of the bright-eyed boys who marched eagerly off to war.

As the years went by, the man's responsibilities grew. He took over more of the farm work as his father's health declined. He began courting a pretty girl from the next town over, stealing kisses by the creek on warm summer evenings. They married in a small ceremony in the town chapel and settled into the rhythms of farm life together.

The decades seemed to blur together - an endless cycle of planting and harvest, births and deaths, joys and sorrows. Children arrived, grew, and left to begin lives and families of their own. The man's once rich brown hair turned grey, then white. His strong hands became gnarled and liver-spotted. But through it all, his marriage remained a constant comfort, a port in the storms of life.

Now in the twilight of his years, the old man spent most of his days sitting on the porch of the farmhouse where he had lived his entire life. His beloved wife had passed on a few years earlier and his children and grandchildren visited when they could, but much of the time he was alone with his memories.

He remembered the laughter of his children echoing across the farm, watching his sons learn to plow a straight furrow and his daughters master his mother's recipes. He remembered the holidays, when the whole clan would gather - the house bursting at the seams with loved ones, the aroma of roasting turkey and freshly baked pies, the peals of laughter and good-natured ribbing. 

He remembered the hard times too. The year a late freeze devastated the orchard crop. Sitting by his mother's bedside as she drew her last breaths. The gut-wrenching worry of waiting for news of his grandson stationed overseas, the euphoria when they learned he would be coming home. Through it all, the joys and the sorrows, his wife had been by his side, his partner, his rock.

The old man sighed, shifting his creaky joints in the worn porch chair. Darkness had settled now, the glittering expanse of the Milky Way stretching across the night sky. He knew his own days were growing short. The doctor said his heart was worn out, beating on borrowed time. He didn't fear death. He had lived a long and full life and he was bone-weary. He was ready to rest.

More than anything, he looked forward to seeing his wife again, to holding her in his arms and telling her how much he loved her and missed her. He hoped there was an afterlife where they would be reunited, where he could see his parents and siblings again, where all the loved ones he had lost over the years would be waiting to embrace him. 

For now, he would enjoy the warm evening breeze and the twinkling stars. He would relish visits with his family, savoring each hug and smile and "I love you". And when the time came, he would close his eyes one last time and slip away, eager to embark on the next great adventure with the one he loved most.

The old man took a deep breath of the honeysuckle-scented air, listening to the crickets and cicadas, an orchestra heralding the arrival of summer. Tilting his head back, he let his eyes trace the ancient patterns of the stars. Somewhere up there were all the mysteries of the universe, the answers to life's questions, the souls of those long passed. 

He felt a deep sense of gratitude wash over him. Gratitude for the life he had been granted, for all the love and laughter, for the strength to weather the storms. What a ride it had been, in all its terrible beauty. 

A shooting star streaked across the sky and the old man closed his eyes, making the same wish he had made for years now. To be reunited with his beloved wife, to feel her hand in his again, to see her brilliant smile and twinkling eyes. He knew that day would come, and he could wait. Until then, he would savor each remaining moment on this earth.

The old man took one last look at the glorious canopy of stars before rising slowly and making his way inside the quiet farmhouse. He knew not what tomorrow would bring, but he would greet it with the same steadfast love and indomitable spirit that had carried him through 90 years. With a smile, he climbed into bed and pulled up the quilt his wife had stitched for them many anniversaries ago. Closing his eyes, he let sleep carry him off, perchance to dream of days gone by and loved ones waiting beyond the veil. And if he did not wake to greet the dawn, he knew that too would be alright. Either way, love would be waiting.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
